items.q: What drives the movement of tectonic plates?
items.correct: Convection currents in the mantle combined with slab pull at subduction zones and ridge push at constructive boundaries.
items.misconception: The Earth's rotation makes the plates move.
items.random_wrong: Plates move because of ocean tides pulling on the crust.
items.why: Students conflate the Earth spinning with crustal motion. Plate motion is driven by heat-driven convection in the mantle, not rotation — rotation only affects things like the Coriolis effect.

items.q: Why are composite volcanoes more explosive than shield volcanoes?
items.correct: Composite volcanoes have high-silica, viscous magma that traps gas, causing pressure to build until violent eruption; shield volcanoes have runny basaltic lava that releases gas easily.
items.misconception: Because composite volcanoes are taller and have more lava.
items.random_wrong: Because composite volcanoes are found in cold climates.
items.why: Students assume size equals explosiveness. The actual driver is magma chemistry — silica content controls viscosity, which controls how gas escapes.

items.q: What is the difference between weather and climate?
items.correct: Weather is short-term atmospheric conditions at a specific time and place; climate is the long-term (typically 30-year) average of weather patterns in a region.
items.misconception: Weather happens outside and climate happens globally.
items.random_wrong: Weather refers to rain and climate refers to temperature.
items.why: Students treat them as different phenomena rather than different timescales. The key distinction is time — climate is statistical, weather is instantaneous.

items.q: What is the enhanced greenhouse effect?
items.correct: The strengthening of the natural greenhouse effect by human activities (mainly burning fossil fuels) which add CO2 and other gases to the atmosphere.
items.misconception: The greenhouse effect is entirely man-made and harmful.
items.random_wrong: It is the hole in the ozone layer letting in more sun.
items.why: The natural greenhouse effect is essential for life — without it Earth would be -18 degrees C. The problem is enhancement. Students also confuse it with the (separate) ozone hole.

items.q: Why do people move from rural to urban areas in LICs?
items.correct: A combination of push factors (rural poverty, lack of services, agricultural change) and pull factors (perceived jobs, education, healthcare) — though reality often disappoints.
items.misconception: Because cities in poor countries are always better than the countryside.
items.random_wrong: Because the government forces them to move to cities.
items.why: Migrants often end up in shanty towns worse than what they left. The decision is based on perception of opportunity, not guaranteed improvement — a key nuance students miss.

items.q: Why is HDI a better measure of development than GNI per capita?
items.correct: HDI combines income, life expectancy and education, capturing quality of life — GNI per capita is just average income and hides inequality and non-economic wellbeing.
items.misconception: Because HDI measures how happy people are.
items.random_wrong: Because HDI is easier to calculate than GNI.
items.why: Students treat HDI as a happiness index. It is broader than GNI but still only captures three measurable dimensions — not subjective wellbeing or environmental quality.

items.q: Why are tropical rainforest soils typically infertile?
items.correct: Heavy rainfall leaches nutrients, and most nutrients are stored in living biomass and rapidly recycled — the soil itself holds very little.
items.misconception: Because so much vegetation grows there, the soil must be fertile.
items.random_wrong: Because rainforest soils are made of clay.
items.why: The lush vegetation hides poor soil. The rainforest works as a tightly closed nutrient cycle; once cleared, the system collapses quickly because the soil cannot sustain growth.

items.q: What is longshore drift?
items.correct: The zigzag movement of sediment along a coast caused by waves approaching at an angle (swash up the beach) and gravity returning water perpendicular (backwash).
items.misconception: Sand moves along the beach because tides pull it sideways.
items.random_wrong: Longshore drift is the rising of sea level over time.
items.why: Students confuse tides with waves. Longshore drift is a wave-driven process — the angle of swash vs straight-down backwash creates the zigzag, not tidal pull.

items.q: How do waves erode a cliff?
items.correct: Through hydraulic action (water compressing air in cracks), abrasion (waves throwing rocks against cliffs), attrition (rocks smashing against each other), and solution.
items.misconception: Waves erode cliffs by pushing water against the rock until it breaks.
items.random_wrong: Cliffs erode because of acid rain dissolving them.
items.why: Water pressure alone is rarely enough. The main mechanisms involve compressed air and rocks acting as tools — students underestimate how erosion needs material to do the wearing.

items.q: Why do meanders migrate across a floodplain?
items.correct: Faster water on the outside of the bend causes erosion (river cliff); slower water on the inside causes deposition (slip-off slope) — over time the channel shifts laterally.
items.misconception: Meanders form because rivers flow around obstacles like rocks.
items.random_wrong: Meanders form because the river is going downhill very steeply.
items.why: Meanders form on gentle gradients, not steep ones, and self-organise rather than dodging obstacles. Students project an intuitive 'water goes around things' model onto a fluid dynamic process.

items.q: What is lag time on a storm hydrograph?
items.correct: The time between peak rainfall and peak river discharge — affected by drainage basin shape, land use, soil saturation and vegetation.
items.misconception: Lag time is how long the storm itself lasts.
items.random_wrong: Lag time is the delay between thunder and lightning.
items.why: Students misread the graph. Lag time is the gap between maximum rain and maximum river flow — it tells you how quickly the basin responds, not the duration of rainfall.

items.q: How do glaciers form U-shaped valleys?
items.correct: Glaciers erode existing V-shaped river valleys through plucking (freezing onto rock and pulling pieces away) and abrasion (rock embedded in the ice scraping the valley floor and sides).
items.misconception: Glaciers carve U-shaped valleys from flat land by pushing through.
items.random_wrong: U-shaped valleys form when rivers freeze and expand.
items.why: Glaciers usually deepen pre-existing river valleys, not create them from nothing. Students miss that glacial landscapes inherit and modify earlier fluvial topography.

items.q: What happens in Stage 2 of the Demographic Transition Model?
items.correct: Death rates fall rapidly (due to better food, sanitation and medicine) while birth rates remain high, causing rapid population growth.
items.misconception: Birth rates rise rapidly because people want larger families.
items.random_wrong: Both birth and death rates fall sharply in Stage 2.
items.why: The population explosion in Stage 2 is driven by falling deaths, not rising births. Students see growth and assume more babies are being born when in fact more babies are surviving.

items.q: Why is an ageing population a challenge for HICs?
items.correct: Rising dependency ratio means fewer workers support more pensioners through tax, increasing pressure on pensions, healthcare and social care services.
items.misconception: Because old people stop contributing anything to society.
items.random_wrong: Because old people consume too much food.
items.why: The issue is structural — the ratio of workers to dependants, not the worth of older people. Students moralise where the geographical concern is about funding flows.

items.q: Why do TNCs locate production in LICs?
items.correct: Lower labour costs, weaker regulation, access to raw materials, large potential markets, and government incentives like tax breaks in special economic zones.
items.misconception: Because TNCs want to develop poorer countries and lift them out of poverty.
items.random_wrong: Because LICs have better infrastructure than HICs.
items.why: Students attribute moral motives. TNCs are profit-driven; any development benefit is a side effect, not a goal — a critical distinction for evaluation questions.

items.q: What causes a tsunami?
items.correct: The sudden vertical displacement of a large volume of water, usually by an undersea earthquake, but also by underwater landslides or volcanic eruptions.
items.misconception: Tsunamis are caused by strong winds over the ocean.
items.random_wrong: Tsunamis are caused by the moon's gravity at full moon.
items.why: Students confuse tsunamis with storm surges or tidal waves. A tsunami is generated by displacement from below, not wind from above — that's why early warning relies on seismic data.

items.q: What is gentrification?
items.correct: The process by which wealthier residents move into a previously low-income urban area, raising property prices and often displacing original residents.
items.misconception: Gentrification is when the government rebuilds an old part of the city.
items.random_wrong: Gentrification means the city becomes more polluted as it grows.
items.why: Students confuse gentrification (private market-led displacement) with regeneration (planned redevelopment). The key feature is who benefits and who is pushed out.

items.q: What is ecological succession?
items.correct: The sequence of changes in a community of organisms over time, from pioneer species colonising bare ground to a stable climax community matched to local climate.
items.misconception: Succession is just one species replacing another randomly.
items.random_wrong: Succession means a forest growing taller each year.
items.why: Succession is directional and predictable, not random. Each stage modifies conditions making the next stage possible — students miss the role of organisms in changing their own environment.

items.q: What is evapotranspiration?
items.correct: The combined loss of water from a surface through evaporation (from soil and water bodies) and transpiration (from plant leaves).
items.misconception: It is only water evaporating from puddles and lakes.
items.random_wrong: It is the process of rain falling from clouds.
items.why: Students focus only on evaporation. Transpiration from forests can move enormous quantities of water — a key concept for understanding deforestation's effect on the water cycle.

items.q: Why do earthquakes of similar magnitude cause different amounts of damage?
items.correct: Damage depends on depth, distance from population, building quality, ground type (liquefaction risk), time of day, and emergency preparedness — not just magnitude.
items.misconception: Bigger magnitude always means more deaths.
items.random_wrong: All earthquakes of magnitude 7 cause the same damage everywhere.
items.why: Magnitude measures energy; impact depends on context. A magnitude 7 in a poor, dense city kills thousands; the same offshore or in a wealthy area may kill no one — a key development comparison.

items.q: What happens during an El Nino event?
items.correct: Trade winds weaken, warm water that normally pools in the western Pacific spreads east, disrupting weather patterns globally — drought in Australia, floods in South America.
items.misconception: El Nino is when the ocean gets colder near Peru.
items.random_wrong: El Nino is the name of a specific hurricane that hit Mexico.
items.why: El Nino brings WARM water to Peru — the cold-water state is La Nina. Students mix up the two phases; the name 'the Christ child' refers to warm currents at Christmas.

items.q: How are fold mountains like the Himalayas formed?
items.correct: By the collision of two continental plates (India and Eurasia) where neither can subduct, so the crust crumples and is forced upwards over millions of years.
items.misconception: Fold mountains form when one plate slides under another into the mantle.
items.random_wrong: Fold mountains form from giant volcanoes erupting upwards.
items.why: Students confuse continent-continent collision with subduction. Continental crust is too buoyant to subduct, so it crumples — distinct from oceanic-continental boundaries like the Andes.

items.q: What does sustainable development mean?
items.correct: Development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs — balancing economic, social and environmental goals.
items.misconception: Sustainable development means stopping all economic growth.
items.random_wrong: Sustainable development means using only solar power.
items.why: Students treat sustainability as anti-development. The Brundtland definition is about balance and intergenerational equity — not zero growth or one specific technology.

items.q: Why are cities warmer than surrounding countryside?
items.correct: Dark surfaces absorb solar radiation, concrete and brick store heat, vehicles and buildings release heat, and lack of vegetation reduces cooling by evapotranspiration.
items.misconception: Because cities are closer to the sun due to tall buildings.
items.random_wrong: Because cities are always in warmer climates.
items.why: The 'closer to the sun' idea reveals misunderstanding of scale — tall buildings are negligible relative to the 150-million-km distance to the sun. The effect is local energy balance.

items.q: What is a drainage basin?
items.correct: The area of land drained by a river and its tributaries, bounded by a watershed (high ground that separates one basin from another).
items.misconception: A drainage basin is the bowl-shaped area at the mouth of the river.
items.random_wrong: A drainage basin is the underground source of a river.
items.why: Students take 'basin' too literally as a bowl. It is the entire catchment area — often a huge region, not a depression near the river itself.

items.q: How does soil form?
items.correct: Through weathering of parent rock, decomposition of organic matter, biological activity, climate influence and time — a slow process taking hundreds to thousands of years.
items.misconception: Soil is just crushed-up rock.
items.random_wrong: Soil is decomposed dinosaurs.
items.why: Soil is a complex mix of mineral and organic matter, water, air and living organisms. Students reduce it to ground rock, missing the crucial biological component.

items.q: What is the difference between a natural hazard and a natural disaster?
items.correct: A natural hazard is a physical event with potential to cause harm; it becomes a disaster only when it affects vulnerable people and causes significant impact.
items.misconception: They are the same thing — any earthquake or hurricane is a disaster.
items.random_wrong: A hazard happens on land and a disaster happens at sea.
items.why: Earthquakes in unpopulated areas are hazards but not disasters. The 'disaster' status depends on human vulnerability — central to modern hazard geography.

items.q: Why do some rivers form deltas and others form estuaries?
items.correct: Deltas form where rivers deposit sediment faster than the sea can remove it (calm seas, heavy load); estuaries form where tidal energy removes sediment (strong tides).
items.misconception: Deltas form on big rivers and estuaries on small ones.
items.random_wrong: Deltas form in cold climates and estuaries in warm climates.
items.why: Size doesn't determine the form. The balance of sediment supply vs marine energy does — the Nile (delta) and Thames (estuary) are similar size but in different sea conditions.

items.q: How do humans affect the carbon cycle?
items.correct: By burning fossil fuels (releasing stored geological carbon to the atmosphere) and deforestation (reducing the biosphere store and releasing carbon).
items.misconception: Humans only affect the carbon cycle by breathing out CO2.
items.random_wrong: Humans affect the carbon cycle by eating plants.
items.why: Human respiration is balanced (we eat carbon that plants captured). The disruption is shifting carbon from long-term geological stores into the active atmospheric cycle.

items.q: What causes global sea level rise?
items.correct: Two main factors: thermal expansion of warming seawater, and the melting of land-based ice (glaciers and ice sheets) adding water to oceans.
items.misconception: Sea level rises mainly because Arctic sea ice is melting.
items.random_wrong: Sea level rises because of underwater volcanoes pushing it up.
items.why: Floating sea ice already displaces its weight in water — its melting does not raise sea level. The contributors are LAND ice (Greenland, Antarctica) and thermal expansion.

items.q: What actually causes earthquakes?
items.correct: The sudden release of built-up stress along faults at plate boundaries as rocks slip past each other, generating seismic waves.
items.misconception: Earthquakes are caused by magma rising up to the surface.
items.random_wrong: Earthquakes are caused by the Earth's core overheating.
items.why: Students conflate volcanic and seismic processes. Most earthquakes are tectonic (stress release at faults), not volcanic — magma movement is a separate process and most quakes occur at non-volcanic boundaries.

items.q: What is the difference between the focus and epicentre of an earthquake?
items.correct: The focus (hypocentre) is the underground point where the earthquake originates; the epicentre is the point on the surface directly above the focus.
items.misconception: The focus and epicentre are the same point.
items.random_wrong: The focus is at the centre of the Earth and the epicentre is on the surface.
items.why: Students treat the two terms as synonymous or place the focus deep at the Earth's core. The focus is just below the epicentre, typically only a few to tens of kilometres deep.

items.q: What happens at a constructive (divergent) plate boundary?
items.correct: Plates move apart, magma rises to fill the gap and solidifies, forming new oceanic crust — for example at the Mid-Atlantic Ridge.
items.misconception: At constructive boundaries the plates collide and build mountains.
items.random_wrong: At constructive boundaries the plates slide past each other sideways.
items.why: Students confuse 'constructive' with 'collision'. Constructive means new crust is constructed; the plates move apart, not together. Sliding sideways is a conservative/transform boundary.

items.q: Why are there no volcanoes at conservative plate boundaries?
items.correct: Conservative boundaries involve plates sliding past each other horizontally — no crust is created or destroyed, so there is no subduction to generate magma.
items.misconception: There are no volcanoes because the boundaries are too cold.
items.random_wrong: There are volcanoes at conservative boundaries — that's where most of them form.
items.why: Volcanism requires magma generation, which needs either subduction or mantle upwelling. Conservative boundaries (like the San Andreas) lack both, but still produce major earthquakes.

items.q: How do hotspot volcanoes differ from subduction-zone volcanoes?
items.correct: Hotspots are stationary mantle plumes that punch through plates anywhere (e.g. Hawaii), while subduction-zone volcanoes form along plate boundaries from melting subducted crust.
items.misconception: Hotspot volcanoes form at plate boundaries like all other volcanoes.
items.random_wrong: Hotspot volcanoes are just very hot subduction volcanoes.
items.why: Hawaii sits in the middle of the Pacific Plate, nowhere near a boundary. The hotspot is fixed while the plate moves over it, producing volcanic chains — a different mechanism entirely.

items.q: Why has the Richter scale been largely replaced by the moment magnitude scale?
items.correct: Richter saturates above magnitude ~7 and was calibrated for local Californian quakes; moment magnitude (Mw) measures the total energy released and works for all sizes globally.
items.misconception: The Richter scale is still the standard scale used by all seismologists.
items.random_wrong: Moment magnitude measures damage while Richter measures shaking.
items.why: Media still says 'Richter' but seismologists now report Mw. Both are logarithmic — a magnitude 7 releases ~32x more energy than a 6 — but Mw is the modern standard for large quakes.

items.q: What is the difference between primary and secondary effects of an earthquake?
items.correct: Primary effects occur immediately as a direct result of shaking (building collapse, deaths); secondary effects follow afterwards (fires, tsunamis, disease, homelessness).
items.misconception: Primary effects are the worst effects and secondary effects are minor.
items.random_wrong: Primary effects happen first and secondary effects happen the next day.
items.why: Secondary effects can be far worse — the 2011 Tohoku tsunami killed more than the shaking itself. The distinction is about cause (direct vs cascading), not severity or strict timing.

items.q: Why do earthquakes typically cause more deaths in LICs than HICs?
items.correct: LICs often have poorly enforced building codes, weak emergency response, dense informal housing and limited preparedness — not because the earthquakes are stronger.
items.misconception: LICs have more earthquake deaths because more earthquakes happen there.
items.random_wrong: HICs never have earthquake deaths because of advanced technology.
items.why: Vulnerability is socio-economic, not seismic. Japan and Haiti both sit on plate boundaries; the death toll difference comes from building quality, planning and response — a key development comparison.

items.q: Why does oceanic crust subduct beneath continental crust?
items.correct: Oceanic crust is denser (basaltic) than continental crust (granitic), so when they collide the denser oceanic plate sinks into the mantle, forming a trench and volcanic arc.
items.misconception: Oceanic crust subducts because the ocean pushes it down with its weight.
items.random_wrong: Continental crust subducts because it is thicker and heavier.
items.why: Density, not water pressure, drives subduction. Continental crust is too buoyant to subduct, which is why continent-continent collisions produce fold mountains instead of trenches.

items.q: What are the three cells of global atmospheric circulation?
items.correct: The Hadley cell (equator to 30 degrees), Ferrel cell (30 to 60 degrees) and Polar cell (60 degrees to poles) together redistribute heat from equator to poles.
items.misconception: There is one big Hadley cell from the equator to each pole.
items.random_wrong: The three cells are tropical, temperate and arctic, named after climate zones.
items.why: Students often learn 'Hadley' as the only cell. There are three in each hemisphere, and their boundaries (at 30 and 60 degrees) explain global pressure belts, deserts and the polar front.

items.q: What weather does an anticyclone (high pressure system) bring?
items.correct: Descending air warms and dries, suppressing cloud formation — so anticyclones bring clear, settled weather: hot in summer, cold and frosty in winter.
items.misconception: High pressure brings stormy weather because the air pushes down hard.
items.random_wrong: Anticyclones bring heavy rainfall because they trap moisture.
items.why: Students conflate 'high pressure' with 'strong weather'. In fact, sinking air evaporates clouds — depressions (low pressure with rising air) bring storms, not anticyclones.

items.q: Why do tropical cyclones only form over warm tropical oceans?
items.correct: They need sea surface temperatures above ~27 degrees C to a depth of 50m to provide energy through evaporation, plus enough Coriolis effect (away from the equator) to spin.
items.misconception: Hurricanes form wherever there are strong winds over the sea.
items.random_wrong: Hurricanes form when two cold fronts collide above the ocean.
items.why: Wind doesn't create hurricanes — warm ocean does. They draw energy from latent heat of evaporating seawater; that's why they weaken rapidly over land or cool water.

items.q: What is the difference between a hurricane and a tornado?
items.correct: Hurricanes are huge (hundreds of km) tropical ocean storms that last days; tornadoes are small (tens of metres) intense rotating columns from thunderstorms over land, lasting minutes.
items.misconception: Hurricanes and tornadoes are basically the same thing.
items.random_wrong: A tornado is just a small hurricane.
items.why: They differ in scale, formation mechanism, location and duration. Hurricanes need warm sea; tornadoes need wind shear in supercell thunderstorms — entirely different processes.

items.q: What is the ITCZ and why does it bring rain?
items.correct: The Intertropical Convergence Zone is where trade winds from both hemispheres converge near the equator, forcing air to rise, cool and produce heavy convectional rainfall.
items.misconception: The ITCZ is a fixed band of permanent storms at the equator.
items.random_wrong: The ITCZ is the boundary between the Atlantic and Pacific oceans.
items.why: The ITCZ shifts seasonally north and south with the sun, driving monsoon rains. Students treat it as static — its movement explains why tropical regions have wet/dry seasons.

items.q: What is the polar jet stream?
items.correct: A fast-flowing river of air ~10km up at the boundary between the Ferrel and Polar cells, where large temperature contrasts create strong winds steering weather systems.
items.misconception: The jet stream is a low-level wind that pushes clouds along.
items.random_wrong: The jet stream is the air left behind by aircraft engines.
items.why: Jet streams sit in the upper troposphere, not at ground level. They steer mid-latitude depressions; meanders (Rossby waves) explain prolonged heatwaves, cold snaps and flooding.

items.q: Why can a cold winter NOT disprove climate change?
items.correct: Climate is the long-term statistical average; a single cold winter is a weather event. Climate change is the trend in averages and extremes over decades, not the absence of cold days.
items.misconception: A really cold winter proves global warming is not happening.
items.random_wrong: Climate change only happens in summer.
items.why: This is one of the most common public misconceptions. Climate is the slope of the trend over 30+ years; individual weather events are scatter around that line — both can be cold.

items.q: How do scientists know current climate change is anthropogenic?
items.correct: Isotopic signatures of CO2 match fossil fuel sources, warming patterns (cooler stratosphere, warmer troposphere) match greenhouse forcing, and natural forcings cannot explain the rate.
items.misconception: Climate change is mainly caused by natural Milankovitch cycles.
items.random_wrong: Climate change is caused by the sun getting hotter.
items.why: Solar output has been flat or slightly declining since 1980 while temperatures rose sharply. Natural cycles operate on tens of thousands of years; the current change is too fast.

items.q: What is a positive climate feedback loop?
items.correct: A process where warming triggers changes that cause more warming — e.g. melting Arctic ice exposes dark ocean, which absorbs more heat, melting more ice (ice-albedo feedback).
items.misconception: Positive feedback means a good outcome for the climate.
items.random_wrong: Positive feedback means the climate balances itself out.
items.why: 'Positive' here means amplifying, not beneficial. Negative feedbacks (which stabilise) are positive for us. The terminology trips students up — it refers to direction of change, not desirability.

items.q: What is a climate tipping point?
items.correct: A threshold beyond which a system changes irreversibly into a new state — e.g. the Amazon flipping from rainforest to savanna, or Greenland's ice sheet committing to total melt.
items.misconception: A tipping point is just when warming reaches 1.5 degrees C.
items.random_wrong: A tipping point is when world leaders agree to act on climate change.
items.why: 1.5 degrees C is a policy target, not a single tipping point. Multiple system-specific thresholds exist, and crossing them can lock in changes that human action cannot reverse on human timescales.

items.q: What is the difference between mitigation and adaptation to climate change?
items.correct: Mitigation reduces the causes (cutting greenhouse gas emissions); adaptation reduces the impacts (e.g. building sea walls, drought-resistant crops, early warning systems).
items.misconception: Mitigation and adaptation mean the same thing — both are about stopping climate change.
items.random_wrong: Mitigation is done by HICs and adaptation is done by LICs.
items.why: The distinction is causes vs consequences. Both are needed: mitigation tackles the source; adaptation copes with already-locked-in change. Many LICs adapt because they can't mitigate global emissions alone.

items.q: What is hydraulic action in a river?
items.correct: The force of moving water itself compressing air into cracks in the river bank or bed, weakening rock until pieces break off — no sediment needed.
items.misconception: Hydraulic action is when rocks in the river smash into each other.
items.random_wrong: Hydraulic action is when the river dissolves rocks chemically.
items.why: Students mix up the four river erosion processes. Hydraulic action = water force; abrasion = water using rocks to scrape; attrition = rocks hitting rocks; solution (corrosion) = chemical dissolving.

items.q: How do rivers transport their load?
items.correct: By traction (rolling large stones along the bed), saltation (bouncing smaller pebbles), suspension (carrying fine sediment in the flow) and solution (dissolved minerals).
items.misconception: Rivers transport everything by pushing it along the bottom.
items.random_wrong: Rivers can only transport material when they are in flood.
items.why: Students miss the four distinct processes. Particle size controls which one applies — and dissolved load is invisible but can be a huge proportion in limestone areas.

items.q: How does erosion change along a river's long profile?
items.correct: Vertical erosion dominates in the steep upper course (cutting down), while lateral erosion dominates in the lower course (widening the floodplain) as gradient decreases.
items.misconception: Rivers erode most powerfully near the sea where they are biggest.
items.random_wrong: Rivers only erode in the upper course and only deposit elsewhere.
items.why: Discharge increases downstream but gradient decreases — net erosion shifts from downcutting to lateral. Deposition occurs in all courses where energy drops, not just at the mouth.

items.q: How does an oxbow lake form from a meander?
items.correct: Erosion on outside bends narrows the meander neck; during a flood the river cuts across the neck, deposition seals the old loop, leaving a horseshoe-shaped lake.
items.misconception: Oxbow lakes form when meanders dry up completely.
items.random_wrong: Oxbow lakes form where two rivers meet and one stops flowing.
items.why: The lake remains water-filled — it's a cut-off loop, not a dry feature. Students miss that the active river abandons the meander after cutting through, and deposition isolates the old channel.

items.q: Why does urbanisation produce a flashier hydrograph?
items.correct: Impermeable surfaces (concrete, tarmac) reduce infiltration, drains speed up runoff to rivers, shortening lag time and increasing peak discharge — raising flood risk downstream.
items.misconception: Urbanisation reduces flood risk because drains take water away safely.
items.random_wrong: Urbanisation makes hydrographs flatter because cities use a lot of water.
items.why: Drains accelerate water to the river rather than removing it — they shift the peak earlier and higher. Urban basins respond faster, giving less warning and higher peak floods.

items.q: What is the difference between hard and soft engineering for flood management?
items.correct: Hard engineering uses built structures (dams, levees, channelisation) to control flow; soft engineering works with natural processes (afforestation, wetland restoration, floodplain zoning).
items.misconception: Hard engineering always works better than soft engineering at preventing floods.
items.random_wrong: Soft engineering means using softer materials like sand instead of concrete.
items.why: Hard engineering is expensive, ecologically damaging and can shift problems downstream. Soft approaches are cheaper and sustainable but slower to act — a typical evaluation question.

items.q: Why does deforestation increase flood risk?
items.correct: Trees intercept rainfall, their roots increase infiltration and transpiration removes water — removing them increases surface runoff and reduces lag time, peaking river levels faster.
items.misconception: Deforestation causes floods because trees normally hold water in their branches forever.
items.random_wrong: Deforestation has no effect on rivers — only rainfall matters.
items.why: Interception is temporary but it delays and reduces runoff. Without forests, water reaches rivers faster and in greater volume — interacting with antecedent soil moisture to drive flood peaks.

items.q: What is the difference between constructive and destructive waves?
items.correct: Constructive waves have strong swash and weak backwash, building beaches; destructive waves have weak swash and strong backwash, dragging sediment offshore and eroding cliffs.
items.misconception: Big waves are destructive and small waves are constructive.
items.random_wrong: Constructive waves build sandcastles and destructive waves wash them away.
items.why: It's the swash-to-backwash balance, not size, that classifies waves. Tall, plunging waves are destructive; long, spilling waves are constructive. Wave frequency also matters.

items.q: Why does longshore drift move sediment in only one direction along a coast?
items.correct: The prevailing wind drives swash diagonally up the beach at an angle, but gravity-driven backwash returns sediment straight downhill — creating net movement in the swash direction.
items.misconception: Longshore drift moves sediment back and forth with the tide.
items.random_wrong: Longshore drift only happens during storms.
items.why: It's wave-driven, not tide-driven. The net direction is set by the prevailing wind angle relative to the coast — that's why groynes are placed perpendicular to interrupt it.

items.q: How do headlands and bays form on a discordant coastline?
items.correct: Less resistant rock (e.g. clay) is eroded faster, forming bays; more resistant rock (e.g. chalk) is eroded slower, left protruding as headlands — perpendicular bands of geology.
items.misconception: Headlands form where the sea bends round to attack the land.
items.random_wrong: Headlands and bays form because of differences in sea temperature.
items.why: The pattern is set by geology (differential erosion of rock bands meeting the coast at right angles), not by water behaviour. Concordant coasts (parallel bands) produce coves, not bays.

items.q: What is the sequence of headland erosion from crack to stump?
items.correct: Crack to cave to arch to stack to stump — waves widen cracks into caves, two caves meet to form an arch, the arch collapses leaving a stack, and the stack erodes to a stump.
items.misconception: Stacks form where rocks fall from the cliff into the sea.
items.random_wrong: Stacks form when the sea level rises and floods a hill.
items.why: It's a sequence of erosion at the same site, not random rockfall or sea level rise. Each landform is the predecessor of the next, with hydraulic action and abrasion driving the progression.

items.q: Why might managed retreat be more sustainable than building a sea wall?
items.correct: Sea walls are expensive, deflect erosion elsewhere and reflect wave energy worsening beach loss; managed retreat works with natural processes, creates habitat and is cheaper long-term.
items.misconception: Sea walls always solve coastal erosion problems permanently.
items.random_wrong: Managed retreat means letting all coastal towns disappear into the sea.
items.why: Sea walls protect one stretch but cause terminal scour and sediment starvation downdrift. Managed retreat selectively allows the coast to adjust — controversial socially but ecologically sound.

items.q: How does a spit form?
items.correct: Longshore drift carries sediment past a change in coastline direction (e.g. river mouth); deposition continues into deeper water, building a curved depositional landform with a hook.
items.misconception: A spit forms where the river deposits its sediment at the coast.
items.random_wrong: A spit is a small island just off the coast.
items.why: Spits are coastal (longshore drift) deposits, not river deposits. The hook forms where wind shifts the current. Students confuse spits with deltas — different processes, different settings.

items.q: What is the difference between glacial abrasion and plucking?
items.correct: Abrasion: rock embedded in the glacier base scrapes the bedrock smooth, creating striations. Plucking: meltwater refreezes onto bedrock, then movement rips chunks of rock away.
items.misconception: Abrasion and plucking are different words for the same process.
items.random_wrong: Abrasion is when wind erodes the glacier and plucking is when rain does.
items.why: Both processes act together but produce different evidence: abrasion leaves smooth scratched surfaces; plucking leaves jagged surfaces. Students conflate them — recognising striations vs roches moutonnees is exam-critical.

items.q: Why do glaciated valleys have a U-shape rather than a V-shape?
items.correct: Glaciers occupy the whole valley width, eroding the sides as well as the floor, producing steep sides and a flat floor (U-shape); rivers erode mainly downwards, leaving V-shape.
items.misconception: Glaciers cut U-shapes because ice is shaped like a U.
items.random_wrong: Glaciers carve V-shaped valleys just like rivers do.
items.why: The U-shape reflects the geometry of erosion (lateral plus vertical), not the shape of the ice itself. A river is confined to a thin channel and erodes vertically — a glacier fills the valley.

items.q: How does a corrie (cirque) form?
items.correct: Snow accumulates in a north-east-facing hollow, compacts to ice, and rotational movement combined with plucking and abrasion deepens the hollow into an armchair shape; meltwater can fill it as a tarn.
items.misconception: A corrie is just a small lake at the top of a mountain.
items.random_wrong: A corrie forms when a river dries up high in the mountains.
items.why: The corrie is the eroded hollow; the tarn (lake) is just the post-glacial water that fills it. Aspect matters — north-east in the Northern Hemisphere because of shade and prevailing wind.

items.q: What is the difference between lateral, medial and terminal moraine?
items.correct: Lateral moraine forms along the sides of a glacier from rockfall; medial moraine forms where two lateral moraines merge; terminal moraine marks the maximum extent at the snout.
items.misconception: Moraines are all the same thing — just piles of rocks.
items.random_wrong: Lateral moraine is the youngest and terminal moraine is the oldest.
items.why: Their position records glacial history. Students lump them as 'rocks'. Distinguishing them is essential — terminal moraines mark past climate maxima; medial moraines reveal tributary glaciers.

items.q: What is the difference between an ice age and a glacial period?
items.correct: An ice age is a long era with permanent ice on Earth (we are still in one); within it, glacial periods (cold) alternate with interglacials (warm) every ~100,000 years driven by Milankovitch cycles.
items.misconception: The ice age ended 10,000 years ago.
items.random_wrong: Ice ages happen every winter when it gets cold.
items.why: We are in an interglacial (the Holocene) within the current ice age — Greenland and Antarctica still have ice sheets. Glacials and interglacials are within ice ages, set by orbital forcing.

items.q: What is the difference between biotic and abiotic components of an ecosystem?
items.correct: Biotic components are all living organisms (plants, animals, microbes); abiotic components are non-living factors (climate, soil, water, light, rock).
items.misconception: Biotic means animals and abiotic means plants.
items.random_wrong: Biotic and abiotic both refer to types of soil.
items.why: Plants are biotic, not abiotic. Students sort by familiarity rather than living vs non-living. The distinction matters because abiotic factors set the conditions for which biotic communities develop.

items.q: Why is a food web a better model than a food chain?
items.correct: A food chain shows one linear path; a food web shows the many interconnected feeding relationships in reality, so it better represents how species depend on multiple food sources.
items.misconception: A food web is the same as a food chain, just drawn differently.
items.random_wrong: A food web shows what spiders eat and a food chain shows what other animals eat.
items.why: Most species have multiple predators and prey. A linear chain is a simplification — the web captures redundancy and resilience. Removing one species rarely collapses a real ecosystem.

items.q: In a tropical rainforest nutrient cycle, where is most of the nutrient stored?
items.correct: In the biomass (living plants). Litter is rapidly decomposed by warm humid conditions, and soil is leached — so cycling between biomass and litter dominates.
items.misconception: Most nutrients in a rainforest are stored in the soil — that's why plants grow so well.
items.random_wrong: Most rainforest nutrients are stored in animals.
items.why: Rainforest soils are notoriously thin and infertile. The Gersmehl model shows biomass as the dominant store — which is why slash-and-burn agriculture collapses quickly when biomass is removed.

items.q: Why does the tropical rainforest have distinct vertical layers?
items.correct: Competition for light: emergents (40m+), canopy (capturing most light), understorey (shade-tolerant) and forest floor (sparse vegetation due to low light) — each adapted to its light level.
items.misconception: Rainforest layers exist because different animals live at different heights.
items.random_wrong: Rainforest layers exist because trees grow in age order tallest to shortest.
items.why: Light availability drives the stratification; animal niches are a consequence. Students reverse cause and effect — the structure is plant-competition-driven, with fauna adapting to existing layers.

items.q: What is the difference between the tundra and the taiga biomes?
items.correct: Tundra is treeless cold biome (too cold and permafrost-dominated for trees), while taiga (boreal forest) is the band of coniferous forest just south of the tundra in subarctic latitudes.
items.misconception: Tundra and taiga are the same biome with different names.
items.random_wrong: Tundra is in the Arctic and taiga is in the Antarctic.
items.why: There is no taiga in the Antarctic because there is no continental land at that latitude. The biomes are distinct (treeless vs forested) — defined by whether the growing season permits trees.

items.q: What maintains the savanna biome's mix of grass and scattered trees?
items.correct: A pronounced wet/dry season prevents continuous forest, frequent fires kill saplings, and large herbivores (e.g. elephants) suppress tree regeneration — maintaining open grass-dominated landscape.
items.misconception: The savanna is dry because it's halfway between rainforest and desert.
items.random_wrong: Savanna trees are scattered because lions eat too many of them.
items.why: It's not just rainfall — fire and herbivory are essential disturbance regimes. Without them, savannas tend to revert to woodland. This is why fire management is central to conservation.

items.q: Why is the Demographic Transition Model criticised for not applying to all countries?
items.correct: It was based on European industrialisation. LICs today face different conditions: imported medicine without industrialisation, cultural factors, gender inequality and globalised economies — not all follow the same path.
items.misconception: All countries follow the five stages of the DTM in order.
items.random_wrong: The DTM only applies to countries with more than 50 million people.
items.why: Many LICs experienced rapid death rate falls without industrialisation, breaking the model's logic. Some HICs are entering a Stage 5 it didn't predict — the DTM is a starting point, not a universal law.

items.q: What is the difference between push and pull factors in migration?
items.correct: Push factors drive people away from their origin (war, poverty, persecution, disaster); pull factors attract people to a destination (jobs, safety, family, healthcare, education).
items.misconception: Push and pull factors are the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Push factors are physical and pull factors are economic.
items.why: Most migration involves both — people leave AND are attracted somewhere. Pure push (refugees) or pure pull (job offers) is rare. The framework helps but oversimplifies real decisions.

items.q: How is the dependency ratio calculated?
items.correct: (Population aged 0-14 plus population aged 65+) divided by population aged 15-64, multiplied by 100 — comparing dependants to the working-age population.
items.misconception: The dependency ratio is the number of children per family.
items.random_wrong: The dependency ratio is the number of unemployed people in a country.
items.why: It compares life-stage groups, not employment status. High ratios can come from many children (LICs) OR many elderly (HICs) — both create burden but very different policy challenges.

items.q: What is the difference between a refugee and an economic migrant?
items.correct: A refugee is forced to flee due to persecution, war or disaster and has legal protections under international law; an economic migrant chooses to move primarily for work or better living standards.
items.misconception: Refugees and economic migrants are basically the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Refugees come from war zones and economic migrants come from rich countries.
items.why: The legal distinction has major consequences — refugees have rights to asylum under the 1951 Convention. Politicians often blur these terms, but they are not interchangeable in policy or geography.

items.q: What is the difference between the Burgess and Hoyt urban land use models?
items.correct: Burgess (1925) describes concentric rings radiating from the CBD; Hoyt (1939) describes sectors (wedges) extending along transport routes — recognising how transport shapes urban growth.
items.misconception: Burgess and Hoyt models show the same thing in different drawings.
items.random_wrong: Burgess is for American cities and Hoyt is for European cities.
items.why: The models differ structurally: rings vs wedges. Both were derived from Chicago. Real cities have elements of both — modern models like Mann combine them with sector wedges within concentric zones.

items.q: What is the main social impact of gentrification?
items.correct: Displacement of long-term lower-income residents as rents and property prices rise, often along with loss of community businesses and cultural identity — even if the area looks 'improved'.
items.misconception: Gentrification only has positive effects because it makes areas nicer.
items.random_wrong: Gentrification means evicting all residents by force.
items.why: Improvements aren't free — they're paid for by displacement. Students focus on visible upgrades and miss the demographic shift. Many evaluation questions ask 'for whom is gentrification beneficial?'.

items.q: What is the difference between a megacity and a world city?
items.correct: A megacity is defined by population (over 10 million); a world city is defined by global economic and cultural influence (e.g. New York, London) — so a city can be one without the other.
items.misconception: Megacities and world cities are the same thing.
items.random_wrong: A megacity has over 1 million people and a world city has over 5 million.
items.why: Dhaka and Kinshasa are megacities but not world cities; Zurich is a world city but not a megacity. The distinction is quantitative (size) vs qualitative (function and global connections).

items.q: Why do informal settlements develop in rapidly growing LIC cities?
items.correct: Rural-urban migration outpaces formal housing supply; migrants build their own homes on marginal land without secure land tenure — driven by housing shortage, not by personal choice.
items.misconception: People live in slums because they prefer them or are too lazy to find better housing.
items.random_wrong: Informal settlements are built by the government for new migrants.
items.why: Self-construction is a response to formal-sector failure. Many residents are employed and pay informal rent. Students moralise; the geographical analysis is about supply, tenure and land economics.

items.q: Why is the urban heat island effect strongest at night?
items.correct: Concrete and brick released their stored daytime heat slowly into the night air, while rural areas cool rapidly through radiative loss — widening the urban-rural temperature gap after sunset.
items.misconception: Urban heat islands are strongest at midday because cities are sunniest then.
items.random_wrong: Urban heat islands only happen in summer.
items.why: Thermal mass of urban materials is the key — they buffer city temperature. At night, this buffering creates the largest urban-rural difference, sometimes 5-10 degrees C in major cities.

items.q: Why are urbanisation rates currently higher in LICs than HICs?
items.correct: HICs urbanised earlier and are now near saturation (80%+ urban); LICs are still in the rapid urbanisation phase, with high rural-urban migration and urban natural increase.
items.misconception: LICs have higher urbanisation rates because they have more cities than HICs.
items.random_wrong: Urbanisation only happens in LICs — HICs are deindustrialising.
items.why: Rate (change) is different from level (proportion urban). HICs have high levels but low rates because most people are already urban. Some HICs even see counter-urbanisation.

items.q: Why is GDP per capita a poor measure of wellbeing?
items.correct: GDP measures economic activity, not wellbeing. It ignores inequality, unpaid work, environmental damage and quality of life — high GDP can coexist with poor health, education or happiness.
items.misconception: A high GDP per capita means citizens are happy and well off.
items.random_wrong: GDP measures the total wealth of every individual in a country.
items.why: Equatorial Guinea has high GDP per capita but very low HDI — the wealth is concentrated. GDP is an average, blind to distribution and non-market activities (parenting, environmental services).

items.q: What is the difference between GDP and GNI?
items.correct: GDP measures all economic activity within a country's borders; GNI adds income from citizens and companies abroad and subtracts income earned by foreigners inside — capturing national income flows.
items.misconception: GDP and GNI are the same — just measured by different organisations.
items.random_wrong: GDP is for the government and GNI is for individuals.
items.why: For countries with large diasporas (e.g. the Philippines), GNI is higher than GDP due to remittances. For TNC-host countries, GNI may be lower than GDP. The distinction matters for development comparison.

items.q: What is the main criticism of Rostow's stages of economic growth?
items.correct: It assumes all countries can follow the same linear Western industrialisation path, ignoring colonial legacies, global trade inequalities and environmental constraints — a top-down modernisation view.
items.misconception: All LICs will pass through Rostow's five stages if they wait long enough.
items.random_wrong: Rostow's model is universally accepted as the best development theory.
items.why: Rostow assumed Western industrialisation as a template, but global economic structures may prevent LICs replicating it. Dependency theorists argue underdevelopment is actively produced — not just a delay.

items.q: What does Frank's dependency theory argue?
items.correct: That LICs (periphery) are kept poor by exploitative trade relationships with HICs (core) — underdevelopment is actively produced by capitalism, not a stage that countries grow out of.
items.misconception: Dependency theory says LICs are poor because they depend too much on foreign aid.
items.random_wrong: Dependency theory is the same as Rostow's modernisation theory.
items.why: Dependency theory is structural and critical, opposing Rostow. The 'dependency' is on the global economic system, not aid. Students confuse the word 'dependency' with aid reliance, missing the political-economic critique.

items.q: What changed between the MDGs (Millennium Development Goals) and the SDGs (Sustainable Development Goals)?
items.correct: MDGs (2000-2015) targeted LICs on basic poverty/health/education; SDGs (2015-2030) apply to all countries and include environmental and inequality goals — broader, more universal scope.
items.misconception: The SDGs replaced the MDGs because the MDGs were achieved.
items.random_wrong: MDGs and SDGs are the same — just renamed.
items.why: Most MDGs were partly achieved but with major gaps. SDGs expanded scope to include HICs (which all face sustainability challenges) and added climate, inequality and oceans — reflecting a shift in development thinking.

items.q: Why is the Brandt Line considered outdated?
items.correct: Drawn in 1980, it predates the rise of NICs (China, India, Brazil) and globalisation — countries like South Korea, Singapore and Chile no longer fit neatly on the 'poor south' side.
items.misconception: The Brandt Line still accurately divides rich and poor countries today.
items.random_wrong: The Brandt Line is the boundary between the Atlantic and Pacific oceans.
items.why: The world has changed: BRICs have grown, some HICs have stagnated. A simple north-south divide cannot capture this. Many geographers prefer the term 'Global North/Global South' loosely or reject the binary altogether.

items.q: What is the difference between outsourcing and offshoring?
items.correct: Outsourcing means contracting work to another company; offshoring means relocating work to another country. A firm can outsource without offshoring, or offshore without outsourcing (in-house overseas).
items.misconception: Outsourcing and offshoring are the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Outsourcing means moving to another country and offshoring means hiring contractors.
items.why: The distinction matters for analysis: outsourcing is about who does the work (firm boundary); offshoring is about where (geography). Many real cases are both — e.g. UK firms outsourcing call centres to Indian contractors.

items.q: What is the 'race to the bottom' critique of globalisation?
items.correct: The argument that TNCs play countries off against each other, relocating to those with lowest wages, weakest regulation and lowest taxes — pressuring governments to cut standards to attract investment.
items.misconception: The race to the bottom means TNCs compete with each other for the lowest prices.
items.random_wrong: Race to the bottom is when companies offshore work to mines and underground factories.
items.why: It's about regulatory competition between states, not price competition between firms. It explains why LICs may keep labour and environmental rules low — competing for FDI rather than improving workers' conditions.

items.q: What is foreign direct investment (FDI)?
items.correct: Cross-border investment where a firm acquires lasting control or ownership of assets in another country (e.g. building a factory, buying a company) — distinct from short-term portfolio investment in shares.
items.misconception: FDI is the same as foreign aid given by governments.
items.random_wrong: FDI is when individuals send money home to their families abroad.
items.why: FDI is private and profit-seeking, not aid (government grants) or remittances (worker income). The control/ownership element distinguishes it from speculative portfolio investment, which can flee rapidly in crises.

items.q: What is glocalisation?
items.correct: The adaptation of global products or services to local tastes, regulations or cultures — e.g. McDonald's offering paneer burgers in India or Tesco selling local foods in different countries.
items.misconception: Glocalisation means the destruction of local cultures by global brands.
items.random_wrong: Glocalisation is a typo of globalisation.
items.why: TNCs glocalise to maximise local sales; it's a strategic response, not cultural homogenisation. Students sometimes describe globalisation effects as glocalisation, missing the active local-adaptation element.

items.q: What is the difference between physical and economic water scarcity?
items.correct: Physical scarcity is insufficient water supply for demand (e.g. arid regions); economic scarcity is inability to access available water due to lack of infrastructure or investment, despite water being present.
items.misconception: Water scarcity only happens in dry countries.
items.random_wrong: Economic scarcity means water is too expensive for governments to buy.
items.why: Sub-Saharan Africa has economic scarcity — there's water but no pipes, pumps or treatment. Students focus on rainfall; the geographical insight is that scarcity is partly an infrastructure/governance issue.

items.q: What are the four pillars of food security?
items.correct: Availability (enough food exists), access (people can obtain it), utilisation (nutritional quality and absorption) and stability (consistent over time) — defined by the FAO.
items.misconception: Food security just means producing enough food.
items.random_wrong: Food security means storing food for emergencies.
items.why: Most modern famines occur where food is available but inaccessible (Amartya Sen's entitlement theory). Production alone doesn't deliver security — distribution, affordability and nutrition all matter.

items.q: Why is 'renewable' not the same as 'clean' energy?
items.correct: Renewable means the source replenishes naturally on human timescales; clean means low environmental/health impact. Hydropower is renewable but floods habitats; bioenergy is renewable but can drive deforestation.
items.misconception: All renewable energy sources are clean and have no environmental impact.
items.random_wrong: Clean energy means it doesn't produce any smoke when burned.
items.why: Large dams cause major ecological and social harm despite being renewable. Nuclear is low-carbon but not renewable. The two categories overlap but don't match — important for energy-policy evaluation.

items.q: Why is the global energy transition slower than many expect?
items.correct: Existing fossil-fuel infrastructure is sunk cost, low-carbon alternatives need vast capital, intermittency requires storage, and political/economic interests resist change — even as costs of renewables fall.
items.misconception: The energy transition is slow because renewable energy doesn't actually work.
items.random_wrong: The transition is slow because people don't want to give up cars.
items.why: Renewables are now often cheaper than fossil fuels per unit. The barriers are infrastructure, finance, grid integration and politics — a system inertia problem, not a technology limitation.

items.q: What drives thermohaline circulation?
items.correct: Differences in seawater density driven by BOTH temperature (thermo) and salinity (haline) — cold, salty water sinks at high latitudes, driving a global ocean conveyor belt over centuries.
items.misconception: Thermohaline circulation is driven only by temperature differences in the ocean.
items.random_wrong: Thermohaline circulation is driven by the wind blowing across the ocean surface.
items.why: Salinity is the other half of the name and the mechanism. Freshwater influx (from melting ice) reduces salinity and could slow the circulation — a key climate change concern (the AMOC weakening).

items.q: What is the Walker circulation and how does ENSO disrupt it?
items.correct: Walker circulation is the east-west tropical Pacific atmospheric loop: trade winds push warm water west, air rises in Indonesia, sinks over Peru. El Nino weakens it, La Nina strengthens it.
items.misconception: El Nino is a current that flows from Peru to Australia.
items.random_wrong: Walker circulation is the rotation of the Earth around the sun.
items.why: Students treat El Nino as a place or current; it's an oscillating state of a coupled ocean-atmosphere system. Walker circulation is the normal state — ENSO refers to its periodic flipping between phases.

items.q: Why is ocean acidification a separate threat from ocean warming?
items.correct: Acidification occurs because oceans absorb atmospheric CO2, forming carbonic acid and lowering pH — this dissolves calcium carbonate shells and corals, independent of any warming.
items.misconception: Ocean acidification is just another name for ocean warming.
items.random_wrong: Ocean acidification is caused by acid rain falling on the sea.
items.why: These are two distinct CO2-driven changes: warming affects heat-sensitive species, acidification affects calcifiers (corals, molluscs, plankton). Even with zero warming, acidification would still threaten marine food webs.

items.q: Why does plastic accumulate in ocean gyres?
items.correct: Gyres are large rotating ocean currents driven by wind and Coriolis effect; floating debris is carried in but the rotation traps it in the centre, where currents are weak, concentrating plastic over decades.
items.misconception: The 'Great Pacific Garbage Patch' is a solid floating island of plastic you can walk on.
items.random_wrong: Plastic accumulates in gyres because the water there is denser.
items.why: The patch is mostly invisible microplastic suspended in the water column, not a continent. The trapping mechanism is fluid-dynamic — Ekman transport into a centre with weak outflow.

items.q: What is coral bleaching?
items.correct: When stressed (usually by warming), corals expel their symbiotic zooxanthellae (algae that provide colour and food), turning white. They can recover if stress eases, but die if it persists.
items.misconception: Coral bleaching is when coral is killed by sunlight bleaching its colour.
items.random_wrong: Coral bleaching is when boats spill chemicals that whiten the reef.
items.why: It's the loss of algal symbionts, not sun-fading. Bleached coral is not yet dead — it's a warning state. Repeated mass bleaching (like 2016 on the Great Barrier Reef) causes long-term reef collapse.

items.q: What is the difference between primary and secondary data in geography fieldwork?
items.correct: Primary data is collected first-hand by the researcher (surveys, measurements, observations); secondary data already exists and is found in sources (census data, maps, published reports).
items.misconception: Primary data is more important and secondary data is less reliable.
items.random_wrong: Primary data is collected in primary schools and secondary data in secondary schools.
items.why: It's about source, not quality. Secondary data (e.g. Census) is often more reliable than small primary surveys. Good fieldwork triangulates both — the distinction is who collected it, not how useful it is.

items.q: What is the difference between qualitative and quantitative data?
items.correct: Quantitative data is numerical and measurable (traffic counts, river width); qualitative data is descriptive and non-numerical (interview quotes, photo descriptions, field sketches).
items.misconception: Quantitative data is always more scientific and reliable than qualitative.
items.random_wrong: Qualitative data means high-quality data and quantitative means a lot of data.
items.why: Both have value: numbers show patterns; words explain why. Mixed-methods studies combine them. Students wrongly assume numbers are objective and words are subjective — both can be done well or poorly.

items.q: What is the difference between random, stratified and systematic sampling?
items.correct: Random: every member has equal chance of selection. Systematic: pick every nth item. Stratified: divide the population into groups and sample proportionally from each — ensures subgroup representation.
items.misconception: All three sampling methods produce the same results.
items.random_wrong: Stratified sampling means picking samples from different strata of rock.
items.why: Stratified sampling is for ensuring subgroups (e.g. age groups, land-use types) are proportionately represented. The choice depends on the population structure — wrong choice gives biased results.

items.q: Why does correlation not imply causation in geographical data?
items.correct: Two variables may correlate because of a third confounding factor, coincidence, or reverse causation — statistical association alone does not prove one causes the other.
items.misconception: If two variables correlate strongly, one must cause the other.
items.random_wrong: Correlation and causation are the same thing.
items.why: Classic example: ice cream sales correlate with drownings — both caused by hot weather, neither causes the other. In geography this matters for evaluating claims like 'urbanisation causes obesity'.

items.q: What is the difference between a four-figure and six-figure grid reference?
items.correct: Four-figure references identify a 1km square (eastings then northings); six-figure references pinpoint a location within that square to 100m accuracy by adding tenths.
items.misconception: Six-figure references are just longer versions of the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Four-figure references are for OS maps and six-figure references are for atlases.
items.why: Accuracy differs by an order of magnitude. Students forget the 'along the corridor and up the stairs' order (eastings before northings) — a common exam error costing easy marks.

items.q: What does a 'large scale' map mean?
items.correct: A large-scale map covers a small area in high detail (e.g. 1:25,000 OS map of one town); a small-scale map covers a large area in less detail (e.g. 1:1,000,000 world atlas).
items.misconception: A large-scale map shows a large area.
items.random_wrong: Large scale means the map is physically big.
items.why: This is counter-intuitive but mathematically logical: 1/25,000 is a larger fraction than 1/1,000,000. Students reverse the terminology and consistently mark themselves down on map skill questions.

items.q: What is the difference between a choropleth and an isoline map?
items.correct: Choropleths use coloured/shaded areas to show data values per region (e.g. population density by county); isoline maps use lines connecting points of equal value (e.g. contours, isobars).
items.misconception: Choropleth and isoline maps are the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Choropleth maps are for showing churches and isoline maps are for showing roads.
items.why: They suit different data types: choropleth for area-based discrete data; isoline for continuous variables. Choosing the wrong one misrepresents the data — a common A-level skills question.

items.q: What does Spearman's rank correlation coefficient measure?
items.correct: The strength and direction of a relationship between two ranked variables. Values range from -1 (perfect negative) through 0 (no correlation) to +1 (perfect positive).
items.misconception: A Spearman value close to 1 proves that one variable causes the other.
items.random_wrong: Spearman's rank measures the average rank of all the data points.
items.why: Spearman shows correlation strength, not causation. Significance testing is also needed to check the result is unlikely by chance — students report rs values without context or significance level.
